prs.title: feat: Added Magic Number using Recursion
prs.body:
Overview
This PR adds a documented C implementation for detecting “magic numbers.”
A magic number, for this context, is a number which, when you repeatedly sum its digits until a single digit remains, results in a final digit of 1.

Details
Functionality:

The isMagicNumber(int n) function checks if the input is a magic number.

Includes printMagicCheck(int n) for demonstration and sample output.

Example usage provided in main().

Algorithm:

Iteratively sum the digits of the number.

Continue reduction until a single digit is left.

If that digit is 1, the original number is magic.

Complexity:

Time Complexity: O(k * log n), where k is the number of digit sum cycles (usually small since each reduces the size of n).

Space Complexity: O(1) (no extra arrays or recursion stack used).

Sample Output:

For 19: outputs "19 is a magic number."

For 123: outputs "123 is NOT a magic number."

For 1001: outputs "1001 is a magic number."

prs.title: Added Tribonnacci Series using Recursion in C
prs.body:
This PR adds a well-documented C implementation for generating the Tribonacci sequence using recursion. The Tribonacci sequence is a generalization of the Fibonacci sequence, where each term is the sum of the previous three terms, beginning with 0, 1, 1.

Details
Functionality:

The tribonacci(int k) function computes the k-th Tribonacci number recursively.

The printTribonacciRecursive(int n) function prints the first n terms of the series.

Example usage is provided in main() for demonstration and easy validation.

Algorithm:

The calculation follows:

T(0) = 0

T(1) = 1

T(2) = 1

T(k) = T(k-1) + T(k-2) + T(k-3), for k >= 3

Complexity:

Time Complexity: O(3^n) due to repeated computations.

Each function call branches into three child calls until the base cases.

Efficient only for small n; not recommended for large n.

Iterative or memoized approaches are better for large sequences.

Space Complexity: O(n), defined by maximum recursion depth.

Sample Output:

For n = 6: 0 1 1 2 4 7

For n = 10: 0 1 1 2 4 7 13 24 44 81

prs.title: feat: added lowest common ancestor problem
prs.body:
1. Brief Description
Find the node that is the lowest (deepest) common ancestor of two given nodes in a binary tree. The algorithm recursively searches left and right subtrees to locate both nodes, and the first node that has both nodes in different subtrees is the LCA.

2. Time Complexity:
O(n) — where n is the total number of nodes in the tree. Each node is visited once.

3. Space Complexity:
O(h) — where h is the height of the tree (depth of recursion stack).

Please mark this PR as hacktoberfest2025 or accepted for Hacktoberfest if possible, so it counts towards my Hacktoberfest participation.

prs.title: Rat in a maze
prs.body:
This PR adds a dynamic, fully commented Java solution for the classic Rat in a Maze problem. Unlike fixed-size solutions, this code works for any rectangular or square maze, allowing users to specify their own maze dimensions. The algorithm uses recursion and backtracking to find a valid path (if one exists) from the top-left to the bottom-right cell, navigating through open cells in the 2D array maze.

Features:
Functions and checks are based on the input maze’s size (rows and columns) — no hardcoded dimensions!

Clear, educational comments for each algorithmic step: safety checks, recursive moves, backtracking, path printing.

Main method with a sample rectangular maze and flexible demo for testing with any user-defined maze.

Supports open source learning and onboarding for new contributors.

Complexity Analysis:
Time Complexity:

Worst Case: O(4^(n*m)) for an n x m maze, due to exploring all possible paths with recursion. (Optimizations can reduce this depending on allowed directions and revisiting rules.)

Space Complexity:

O(n*m) for the solution and recursion stack.

Usage:
Define any maze (as a 2D array of 1s and 0s) in main() to test and visualize solutions.

The path (“solution matrix”) is printed to the console, marking the traversed cells.

Please mark this PR as hacktoberfest2025 or accepted for Hacktoberfest if possible, so it counts towards my Hacktoberfest participation.

prs.title: feat: Added Bucket sort implementation
prs.body:
Bucket Sort Algorithm Implementation in Java

This PR adds a thoroughly documented Java implementation of the Bucket Sort algorithm. Bucket Sort is a comparison sort algorithm that distributes elements into several buckets, sorts each bucket individually (using Java’s built-in sort), and then concatenates the sorted buckets to produce the final sorted array. The code is designed for arrays of floating point numbers uniformly distributed in

Features:
Modular bucketSort() function with clear stepwise comments: distribution, per-bucket sorting, and merging

Example usage via a main() method

Readable, open-source ready structure targeted for collaborative education and onboarding

Commented time and space complexity

Complexity Analysis:
Time Complexity:

Best/Average Case (Uniform input): O(n), where n is the number of elements (bucket distribution and insertion; in-practice per-bucket sorts are small and fast)

Worst Case: O(n²), if all elements end up in a single bucket (rare with uniform data; falls back to the sorting algorithm used within the buckets, here Java's Timsort)

Space Complexity:

O(n + k), where n is input size and k is the number of buckets (typically k ≈ n)

Usage:
Edit the data array in main() to test with your own input

Run to see bucket sort in action (before/after output shown)

Could you please label this PR with hacktoberfest2025 or mark it as accepted for Hacktoberfest?
This will help my contribution count towards Hacktoberfest 2025 participation.

prs.title: Sudoku Solver using C
prs.body:
This PR introduces a well-documented C implementation of a Sudoku Solver utilizing the backtracking algorithm.

The program takes a 9x9 Sudoku grid as input and recursively fills empty cells by trying all possible values from 1 to 9.

For each empty cell, it checks if a value is valid by ensuring it does not violate the constraints of rows, columns, and 3x3 boxes.

If a conflict is found, the algorithm backtracks and tries the next possible value.

The solver guarantees a solution if one exists and prints the solved grid, or notifies if no solution is possible.

Example use:
Input: Standard 9x9 grid with zeros representing empty cells

Output: Completed Sudoku grid if solvable

prs.title: feat:solution of PathSum
prs.body:
This PR implements a clean and well-commented Java solution for the “Path Sum” problem (LeetCode #112).

The code checks whether a binary tree contains a root-to-leaf path such that the sum of the node values equals a given targetSum.

Includes TreeNode class definition, the hasPathSum method, and an example usage with the sample tree from the problem statement.

Solution uses recursion for efficient traversal and provides full documentation within the code.

Example
Input: root = [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2,null,null,null,1], targetSum = 22

Output: true

Explanation: The path 5→4→11→2 sums to 22.

Additional Notes
Time complexity: O(n), where n is the number of nodes.

Space complexity: O(h), where h is the height of the tree.

Fully tested with sample input and ready for review.

prs.title: Changed-color-of-text-in-footer
prs.body:
# Pull Request Template

## Description

Please include a summary of the change and which issue is fixed. Also include relevant motivation and context.

## Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Documentation update
- [x] Other (please describe)

## Related Issue
Closes #

## Checklist
- [ ] My code follows the style guidelines of this project
- [ ] I have performed a self-review of my code
- [ ] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [ ] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [ ] My changes generate no new warnings
- [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective or that my feature works
- [ ] New and existing unit tests pass locally with my changes
